corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: In future work, it may be fruitful to investigate if the two components can be jointly pre-trained from scratch, either with a denoising objective similar to BART or some another objective.
We propose two models that marginalize over the latent documents in different ways to produce a distribution over generated text.
In the following, we formally introduce both models and then describe the p η and p θ components, as well as the training and decoding procedure.

For further improvement, BART and T5 propose a single, pre-trained encoder-decoder model that leverages bi-directional attention to achieve stronger performance on discriminative and generative tasks.
Our work aims to expand the space of possible tasks with a single, unified architecture, by learning a retrieval module to augment pre-trained, generative language models.

These successes leverage different retrieval-based architectures and optimization techniques to achieve strong performance on a single task, while we show that a single retrieval-based architecture can be fine-tuned for strong performance on a variety of tasks.
We included some gold sentences in order to assess the accuracy of the annotators.
For TriviaQA, this test dataset is the public TriviaQA Web Development split.
Large pre-trained language models have been shown to store factual knowledge in their parameters, and achieve state-of-the-art results when fine-tuned on downstream NLP tasks.
We introduce RAG models where the parametric memory is a pre-trained seq2seq model and the non-parametric memory is a dense vector index of Wikipedia, accessed with a pre-trained neural retriever.

We fine-tune and evaluate our models on a wide range of knowledgeintensive NLP tasks and set the state of the art on three open domain QA tasks, outperforming parametric seq2seq models and task-specific retrieve-and-extract architectures.
For language generation tasks, we find that RAG models generate more specific, diverse and factual language than a state-of-the-art parametric-only seq2seq baseline.

While this development is exciting, such models do have downsides: They cannot easily expand or revise their memory, can't straightforwardly provide insight into their predictions, and may produce "hallucinations" .
Crucially, by using pre-trained access mechanisms, the ability to access knowledge is present without additional training.

Our RAG models achieve state-of-the-art results on open Natural Questions , WebQuestions and CuratedTrec and strongly outperform recent approaches that use specialised pre-training objectives on TriviaQA .

Our RAG models achieve state-of-the-art results on open Natural Questions , WebQuestions and CuratedTrec and strongly outperform recent approaches that use specialised pre-training objectives on TriviaQA .
Despite these being extractive tasks, we find that unconstrained generation outperforms previous extractive approaches.
For FEVER fact verification, we achieve results within 4.3% of state-of-the-art pipeline models which use strong retrieval supervision.

Our work unifies previous successes in incorporating retrieval into individual tasks, showing that a single retrieval-based architecture is capable of achieving strong performance across several tasks.
This said, RAG techniques may work well in these settings, and could represent promising future work.
We showed that our RAG models obtain state of the art results on open-domain QA.

downstream_contract.score_warning: Scores are ranking and filtering signals, not calibrated probabilities.
